questions.stem: Which of the following sentences is a statement (proposition), that is, a declarative sentence that is definitely either true or false?
questions.explanation: A statement must be declarative and carry exactly one truth value. The question and the request make no claim that can be true or false, so neither is a statement. The equation $2x - 1 = 9$ holds only for $x = 5$ and fails otherwise, so its truth depends on the free variable; it is an open sentence, not a statement. The sentence asserting that $5$ is prime is declarative and definitely true, so it is the statement.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $p$ be the statement: it is raining. Which symbolic form represents the sentence: it is not the case that it is raining?
questions.explanation: The phrase it is not the case that reverses the truth value of the statement it qualifies, which is exactly what negation does. So the sentence is $\lnot p$, true precisely when $p$ is false. The conjunction $p \land p$ and disjunction $p \lor p$ are both logically equivalent to $p$ itself, and $p \to p$ is always true, so none of them reverse the truth value of $p$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: If $p$ is true and $q$ is false, what is the truth value of the conjunction $p \land q$?
questions.explanation: A conjunction $p \land q$ is true only when both conjuncts are true and false in every other case. Here $q$ is false, so the conjunction fails regardless of $p$. The truth value is therefore false, with no dependence on context.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: If $p$ is false and $q$ is true, what is the truth value of the disjunction $p \lor q$?
questions.explanation: The inclusive disjunction $p \lor q$ is true when at least one disjunct is true and false only when both are false. Here $q$ is true, so the disjunction is true even though $p$ is false. A single true disjunct is enough to make the whole disjunction true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: The conditional $p \to q$ is false in exactly one combination of truth values. Which one?
questions.explanation: A conditional promises that whenever the antecedent holds the consequent holds too, so it is broken only when the antecedent is true yet the consequent is false. That single combination, $p$ true and $q$ false, makes $p \to q$ false. In all three other rows the conditional is true: whenever the antecedent is false the conditional is vacuously true, and when both are true the promise is kept.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: What is the truth value of the conditional $p \to q$ when $p$ is false and $q$ is false?
questions.explanation: A conditional with a false antecedent is true by definition, no matter what the consequent does; logicians call this a vacuously true conditional. Since $p$ is false here, $p \to q$ is true even though $q$ is also false. The only way to falsify a conditional is a true antecedent paired with a false consequent, which does not occur here.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: The biconditional $p \leftrightarrow q$ is true in which of the following cases?
options: Exactly when $p$ and $q$ have opposite truth values
Exactly when $p$ and $q$ have the same truth value
questions.explanation: The biconditional $p \leftrightarrow q$ means $p$ if and only if $q$, asserting that the two statements share a truth value. It is therefore true in the two rows where both are true or both are false, and false in the two rows where they differ. Matching truth values, not a particular one being true, is what makes it hold.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A truth table for a compound statement built from the three variables $p$, $q$, and $r$ has how many rows?
questions.explanation: Each variable can independently take one of two truth values, true or false, so $n$ variables produce $2^n$ distinct rows. With three variables the table has $2^3 = 8$ rows, one for each combination. The values $6$ and $9$ come from mistakenly multiplying or squaring the count of variables rather than raising two to that power.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: In the four-row truth table for the conjunction $p \land q$, in how many rows is the statement true?
questions.explanation: A conjunction is true only when both parts are true, which happens in a single row: $p$ true and $q$ true. The other three rows (TF, FT, FF) each have at least one false conjunct, so the conjunction is false there. Hence exactly one of the four rows makes $p \land q$ true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: In the four-row truth table for the inclusive disjunction $p \lor q$, in how many rows is the statement true?
questions.explanation: An inclusive disjunction is false only when both disjuncts are false, which is the single row $p$ false and $q$ false. The remaining three rows (TT, TF, FT) each contain at least one true disjunct, so the disjunction is true in all of them. That gives three rows where $p \lor q$ is true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A compound statement that is true for every assignment of truth values to its variables is called a:
questions.explanation: A tautology is a statement whose truth-table column is true in every row, such as $p \lor \lnot p$. A contradiction is the opposite, false in every row, while a contingency is true in some rows and false in others. The converse is a related conditional, not a classification by truth values, so it does not fit.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Which of the following compound statements is a contradiction, that is, false for every assignment of truth values?
questions.explanation: A contradiction is false in every row of its truth table. The statement $p \land \lnot p$ asks for $p$ and its negation to be true at once, which is impossible, so it is false whether $p$ is true or false. By contrast $p \lor \lnot p$ and $p \to p$ are tautologies, and $p \lor q$ is true in three of its four rows, so none of those is a contradiction.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: By De Morgan's laws, the negation $\lnot(p \land q)$ is logically equivalent to:
questions.explanation: De Morgan's first law states $\lnot(p \land q) \equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q$: negating a conjunction negates each part and switches the connective from and to or. A conjunction fails as soon as either part fails, so its denial only requires one of the parts to be false, matching the disjunction of the negations. The form $\lnot p \land \lnot q$ is the negation of $p \lor q$ instead, so it is the common trap of keeping the connective unchanged.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: By De Morgan's laws, the negation $\lnot(p \lor q)$ is logically equivalent to:
questions.explanation: De Morgan's second law states $\lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$: the denial of a disjunction requires both disjuncts to be false. A disjunction is true if even one part holds, so denying it forces every part to fail, giving the conjunction of the negations. Choosing $\lnot p \lor \lnot q$ leaves the connective as or and is the negation of $p \land q$ rather than of $p \lor q$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: The contrapositive of the conditional $p \to q$ is:
questions.explanation: The contrapositive of $p \to q$ is formed by negating both parts and reversing them, giving $\lnot q \to \lnot p$. It is logically equivalent to the original conditional, sharing the same truth-table column in all four rows. The form $q \to p$ is the converse and $\lnot p \to \lnot q$ is the inverse; neither is in general equivalent to $p \to q$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Which conditional is the converse of $p \to q$?
questions.explanation: The converse of $p \to q$ swaps the antecedent and consequent, producing $q \to p$. The converse is not logically equivalent to the original conditional in general, which is why affirming a converse is a recognised reasoning error. The form $\lnot q \to \lnot p$ is the contrapositive (which is equivalent) and $\lnot p \to \lnot q$ is the inverse.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: The conditional $p \to q$ is logically equivalent to which of the following disjunctions?
questions.explanation: The standard equivalence is $p \to q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$. To check, note both are false only when $p$ is true and $q$ is false: that is the one row falsifying the conditional, and the one row where $\lnot p$ is false and $q$ is false so the disjunction also fails. In every other row both expressions are true, so the columns agree.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $p$ be true, $q$ be false, and $r$ be true. What is the truth value of $(p \land q) \lor r$?
questions.explanation: Evaluate the inner conjunction first: $p \land q$ with $p$ true and $q$ false is false. The whole expression is then $\text{false} \lor r$, and since $r$ is true the disjunction is true. A true disjunct on either side makes the disjunction true, so the value is true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Two statements are logically equivalent when:
options: they have the same number of variables
their truth tables agree in every row
one is the negation of the other
questions.explanation: Logical equivalence, written $\equiv$, means two statements have identical truth values under every assignment, so their truth-table columns match row by row. Sharing the same variables or connectives is neither necessary nor sufficient. If one statement were the negation of the other their columns would be exact opposites, which is the opposite of equivalence.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Which pair of statements is logically equivalent (the double negation law)?
questions.explanation: Negating a statement twice restores its original truth value, so $\lnot(\lnot p) \equiv p$; this is the double negation law. If $p$ is true then $\lnot p$ is false and $\lnot(\lnot p)$ is true again, and similarly when $p$ is false, so the columns match. The other pairs differ in at least one row, so they are not equivalent.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Using the equivalence $p \to q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$, the negation $\lnot(p \to q)$ is logically equivalent to:
questions.explanation: Start from $p \to q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$ and negate using De Morgan: $\lnot(\lnot p \lor q) \equiv \lnot(\lnot p) \land \lnot q \equiv p \land \lnot q$. This matches the intuition that a conditional fails exactly when the antecedent holds but the consequent does not, that is, $p$ true and $q$ false. The trap is forgetting to flip the connective when negating the disjunction.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: What is the truth value of $p \to (q \to p)$ when $p$ is false and $q$ is true?
questions.explanation: The outer conditional has antecedent $p$, which is false, and a conditional with a false antecedent is true regardless of its consequent. So $p \to (q \to p)$ is true here without even evaluating the inner part. In fact $p \to (q \to p)$ is a tautology, true in every row, because whenever $p$ is true the inner $q \to p$ has a true consequent and whenever $p$ is false the outer conditional is vacuously true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: A compound statement is built from the four variables $p$, $q$, $r$, and $s$. Find how many rows its truth table has, and in how many of those rows the conjunction $p \land q \land r \land s$ is true.
questions.solution: Each of the four variables is independently true or false, so the number of rows is {{1}}. A conjunction of all four variables is true only when {{2}}, which describes exactly {{3}} of those rows.

options: every one of the four variables is true
at least one of the four variables is true
every one of the four variables is false
an even number of the variables are true
gaps.answer: every one of the four variables is true

questions.explanation: Each variable independently takes one of two truth values, so $n$ variables give $2^n$ rows; here $2^4 = 16$. The trap $2 \times 4 = 8$ multiplies instead of exponentiating, $4! = 24$ counts orderings, and $4^4$ raises the wrong base to the wrong power. A conjunction is true only when every conjunct is true, the single row with $p$, $q$, $r$, $s$ all true, so exactly $1$ of the $16$ rows makes it true. The distractor "at least one variable is true" describes when the disjunction, not the conjunction, holds.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Consider the implication $p \to q$. Across its full four-row truth table, reason about where it is true and where it is false.
questions.solution: A conditional is false in exactly the row where {{1}}. That is a single row, so of the four rows the conditional is true in the other {{2}}. This is also why, whenever the antecedent $p$ is false, the conditional comes out {{3}} no matter what $q$ is.
questions.explanation: A conditional $p \to q$ breaks its promise only when the antecedent holds but the consequent fails, that is $p$ true and $q$ false, which is one row. The other three rows make it true, so it is true in $3$ of the $4$ rows. In particular a false antecedent gives a vacuously true conditional regardless of $q$, so the last blank is true, not "equal to $q$". The remaining row options ($p$ false with $q$ true, both false, both true) are all rows where the conditional actually holds.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Apply De Morgan's law, then classify two basic compound statements by their truth tables.
questions.solution: By De Morgan's law, $\lnot(p \lor q)$ is logically equivalent to $\lnot p$ {{1}} $\lnot q$. Turning to classification: since a statement and its negation are never simultaneously true, $r \land \lnot r$ is a {{2}}, while $r \lor \lnot r$, where at least one side always holds, is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: De Morgan's law gives $\lnot(p \lor q) \equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q$: the OR becomes an AND, and keeping it as OR is the classic error that instead negates $p \land q$. A statement ANDed with its own negation, $r \land \lnot r$, is false in every row, a contradiction; $r \lor \lnot r$ is true in every row, a tautology (the law of the excluded middle). Swapping these two is the common mistake, since a variable AND its negation is $0$ but a variable OR its negation is $1$. A contingency would be true in some rows and false in others, which neither of these is.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)

questions.stem: Let $p$ be true, $q$ be false, and $r$ be false. Evaluate $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$, and identify the disjunction that is logically equivalent to the conditional $p \to q$.
questions.solution: The left side $p \lor q$ evaluates to {{1}}, since a disjunction needs only one true disjunct. With $r$ false, the right side $\lnot r$ is true, and because an AND is true exactly when both sides are true, $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$ is {{2}}. Separately, the standard equivalence rewrites the conditional as $p \to q \equiv$ {{3}}.

options: true only if both $p$ and $q$ hold

questions.explanation: A disjunction is true as soon as one disjunct is true, so $p \lor q$ is true here because $p$ is true, even though $q$ is false; the trap "true only if both hold" confuses OR with AND. With $r$ false, $\lnot r$ is true, and true AND true is true, so $(p \lor q) \land \lnot r$ is true. Finally, the conditional equivalence is $p \to q \equiv \lnot p \lor q$, which fails only when $p$ is true and $q$ is false, matching the single row where $\lnot p \lor q$ is false. The trap $p \lor \lnot q$ is instead equivalent to the converse $q \to p$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Discrete Mathematics course (CSM 165 / MATH 172)
